verification.note: Section 207(2)'s four conditions (individual, resident, 60+ at any time in the year, no PGBP income) are long-standing and confirmed against the Income Tax Department's own tutorial (as amended by Finance Act 2025). The exemption is gated by income type, not amount - it is not capped by the size of non-business income. Section 115BAC(6)'s regime-switching mechanics for persons with business/professional income were confirmed against the Income Tax Department's own Form 10-IEA FAQ page and the statutory first proviso to 115BAC(6) (quoted in full by TaxGuru): it is a one-time reversal (old-regime taxpayer may switch back to the new regime exactly once, after which they are locked out of the old regime for good unless they cease to have business income) - not a lifetime cap on choosing the old regime, and not literally 'once in a lifetime' as commonly and imprecisely summarised. Re-check only if a Budget/Finance Act amendment to Section 207, 115BAC, or 43(5) is reported.

source_refs: Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 207(2) (advance tax exemption for resident senior citizens without business/professional income)
Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 208 (liability to pay advance tax)
Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 43(5) (definition of speculative transaction - intraday equity is speculative business, F&O is non-speculative business)
Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 115BAC(6) and its first proviso (option to opt out of new regime for business/professional-income taxpayers, and its one-time withdrawal)
https://incometaxindia.gov.in/tutorials/32-%20exemption%20from%20pymt%20of%20adv.%20tax.pdf (Income Tax Department official tutorial, as amended by Finance Act 2025)
https://www.incometax.gov.in/iec/foportal/help/statutory-forms/popular-form/form-10-IEA-faq (Income Tax Department official Form 10-IEA FAQ, see Q12/Q14/Q20/Q21)
https://taxguru.in/income-tax/section-115bac6-opting-out-new-tax-regime.html (quotes the statutory proviso to 115BAC(6) in full)
https://cleartax.in/s/fo-trader-return-filing (F&O as non-speculative business income under Section 43(5))
rules/advance-tax.json (the 234B/234C calculation this exemption feeds into)

no_business_income.how_exercised: selected directly in ITR-1/ITR-2 by the Section 139(1) due date, no separate form

opting_into_old_regime.deadline: on or before Section 139(1) due date for that assessment year
opting_into_old_regime.note: once filed to opt for the old regime, does not need to be re-filed every subsequent year to remain on the old regime

reverting_to_new_regime.after_reversal: permanently ineligible to opt for the old regime again while business/professional income continues

has_business_or_professional_income_incl.exception_restoring_free_choice: if the person ceases to have any business or professional income in a later year, they revert to the 'no business income' category and regain the free annual regime choice
has_business_or_professional_income_incl.common_mischaracterization: described online as 'switch old/new only once in a lifetime' - the precise rule is a one-time reversal from old back to new regime, not a lifetime cap on ever choosing the old regime, and not a symmetrical once-each-direction rule
has_business_or_professional_income_incl.statutory_basis: Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 115BAC(6), first proviso

values.compounding_scenario_note: A resident senior citizen (60+) who has only pension/salary/capital-gains/other-sources income gets both the Section 207(2) advance-tax exemption and a free annual regime choice. The moment they acquire any business or professional income - including from intraday/speculative or F&O trading - both benefits are lost together for that year: they become subject to ordinary advance-tax/234B/234C rules, and their regime choice becomes governed by the Section 115BAC(6) one-time-reversal mechanic instead of a free annual pick. Neither loss is age-related; both are triggered purely by the presence of business income.
